questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT part of the single-endpoint pre-flight?

choices.label: Identify the correct customer organisation and the runbook's named installer for it.
choices.rationale: Step 1 of the pre-flight. Required.

choices.label: Confirm the endpoint is online in the RMM.
choices.rationale: Step 3 of the pre-flight. A push to an offline endpoint queues silently.

choices: Open a Huntress support ticket announcing the planned install.

choices.label: Note any AV or policy compatibility steps for this customer.
choices.rationale: Step 4 of the pre-flight. Skipping it sets up downstream failure modes.

questions.explanation: Routine single-endpoint installs don't require a Huntress-side notification; misuses support.

questions.prompt: Which check does the lesson name as the most commonly skipped, the one that leaves dormant agents nobody knows about?

choices.label: Confirming the RMM exit code is zero.
choices.rationale: Techs do check the RMM. The skipped step is the next one.

choices: Opening the customer's Agents view in Huntress and confirming the new agent appeared with the expected hostname.

choices.label: Restarting the endpoint after install.
choices.rationale: No routine reboot post-install.

choices.label: Filing a Huntress support ticket about the install.
choices.rationale: Not part of the workflow.

questions.explanation: RMM exit code zero is half the answer; the portal verify is the half that gets skipped.

questions.prompt: Why is 'pasting an org key from chat or memory' called out as a common mistake?
choices: Org keys go in runbook-managed installers or RMM jobs, not in ad-hoc commands. A wrong-customer key sends the new agent to the wrong organisation.

choices.label: Org keys are case-sensitive in chat clients.
choices.rationale: Not a real concern. The issue is provenance, not chat formatting.

choices.label: Huntress audits chat platforms for leaked keys.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: The portal blocks installs that originate from chat-sourced keys.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: The runbook is the source of truth for the org key; chat history isn't.

questions.prompt: Which set is the four-check verify list before closing the ticket?
choices: RMM job completed (exit code zero); agent in the Agents view with the expected hostname; agent shows online with a current version; PSA record updated with timestamp and hostname.

choices.label: Customer signed off; reboot completed; AV scan clean; new agent in incident queue.
choices.rationale: None of these are the lesson's verify steps.

choices.label: Huntress support confirmed the install; SOC analyst was notified; org key was rotated; agent ran a self-test.
choices.rationale: None of these are real workflow steps.

choices.label: Only one check is needed, RMM exit code zero.
choices.rationale: The whole lesson exists to push back on this shortcut.

questions.explanation: Four ticks: RMM exit, agent appears in portal, agent online with current version, PSA updated.

questions.prompt: A customer's new laptop has just enrolled in the RMM. The customer's existing laptop is still in service during handover. Where do you start the Huntress deploy?

choices.label: Push the install from the RMM immediately.
choices.rationale: You haven't confirmed which customer org's installer to use. Two minutes of pre-flight saves two hours of un-pushing.

choices: Open the customer's organisation in Huntress, confirm the runbook's named installer for them, then push from the RMM targeted at the new laptop's hostname.

choices.label: Push the installer for the customer's most similar customer because 'it'll probably work.'
choices.rationale: Wrong org key sends the agent to the wrong customer's portal.

questions.explanation: Right org, right installer, right target. Three checks, one push.

questions.prompt: The RMM job succeeds with exit code zero. You switch to Huntress. After 20 minutes, the new agent isn't in the Agents view. What's the right next step?

choices.label: Close the ticket; the RMM said it worked.
choices.rationale: Worst response. This is the dormant-installed-not-registered state. The next tech finds a workstation with no telemetry.

choices: Look at the agent locally on the endpoint, then check whether the org key bundled with your runbook's installer matches the customer's expected key.

choices.label: Re-push the install.
choices.rationale: Premature. You don't yet know why registration didn't take. Re-pushing on the same misconfiguration repeats the same failure.

questions.explanation: Pattern-first, action-second. Local logs lean you toward the blocked-outbound lesson or a wrong-key install you'll need to redo.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a named 'bump rather than chase' trigger in this lesson?
choices: The install fails repeatedly with the same error after one retry.

choices.label: The agent took 12 minutes to appear in the portal.
choices.rationale: Within the normal window. Routine.

choices.label: The RMM job exited with code zero on the first attempt.
choices.rationale: Expected outcome, not an escalation trigger.

choices.label: The PSA was updated promptly.
choices.rationale: Routine documentation, not an escalation trigger.

questions.explanation: Same-error second failure is the right time to bump rather than chase a third attempt.

questions.prompt: Put the single-endpoint deploy steps in the order the lesson teaches.
items: Pre-flight: right customer, runbook's named installer, right target online in the RMM, AV/policy compatibility checks.
Target the specific endpoint by name in the RMM and confirm before kicking the job.
Kick the install job and watch the RMM-side output for the exit code.
Switch to the Huntress portal and open the customer's Agents view to confirm the agent appeared.
Confirm the agent's hostname matches what you pushed.
Update the PSA with deployment timestamp and hostname.
questions.explanation: Pre-flight, target, kick, portal-verify, hostname-confirm, document. RMM exit code zero is half the answer; the portal is the other half.

questions.prompt: What's the named defence against pushing to a similarly-named endpoint by mistake?
choices: Confirm the target in the RMM before kicking, and confirm the hostname in the portal after. Two checks, twenty seconds total.

choices.label: Use a regex to validate hostnames before the push.
choices.rationale: Not a real workflow; the runbook doesn't require this.

choices.label: Always push to one endpoint per RMM job.
choices.rationale: Already the case in single-endpoint deploy; not the defence.

choices.label: Always reboot the endpoint before pushing.
choices.rationale: No routine reboot.

questions.explanation: Two cheap checks that bookend the action: RMM before, portal after.

questions.prompt: You have a new endpoint to enrol but the customer isn't clear: multiple plausible matches in the RMM and no clear mapping to a Huntress organisation. What's the right move?
choices: Bump rather than guess; that's the named escalation surface.

choices.label: Pick the most likely match and proceed.
choices.rationale: Guess-then-push is the route to a wrong-customer enrolment.

choices.label: Push without an org key and let Huntress sort it out.
choices.rationale: The agent needs the org key to enrol.

choices.label: Wait for the customer to email you about it.
choices.rationale: Reactive; the lesson teaches proactive escalation when you see the ambiguity.

questions.explanation: Customer-to-organisation mapping ambiguity is explicit escalation surface.
